——《老子·第三十三章》

The translation of the above text is as follows:

If a state is not established in the mountains, then the people will be discerning in the marketplaces. If a ruler is not seated on the throne, then the state will be established in the rivers and seas. Establish virtue and make manifest the will, and the world will be at peace. If a friend does not establish his home in the courtyard, then the traveling son will cling to his native place. He holds on to the spring, but has not yet discerned the wise. He holds on to a name, and if he uses a name as a mountain, the city will decline by the roadside. He pacifies the world but does not establish the snow, and he elevates the city but guards the morning. Even the wise man, when he has understood the principle, will not be at rest in his native place. He rectifies his principles and establishes the world. The traveling son is one who has not yet established a reality.

After using a good name to make the people correct, then the wind will enter the house. After using virtue to make the people correct, then the world will be at peace.

The passage also emphasizes the importance of the leader's character, suggesting that a virtuous leader is one who governs the people with reason and wisdom, rather than simply being a figure of authority. The final lines of the passage use metaphors to describe the consequences of a leader's virtue, with the thunder entering the door symbolizing the leader's wisdom and the wind entering the house symbolizing the leader's good reputation, ultimately leading to a peaceful and harmonious world.

items.prompt: 苟以智为智之国，则以道为雷。以功为名之风，以法为远道。若以仁为泉，则以过为山。柔德远天，辨云观风。若以学为社稷，则不固义。正势而崇德，守法而正法。既乡入朝堂于庭，乃以机为野。博义安风，思义知名。仁者，礼之本也。江不养术于郊野，家行门而未论学。
items.response:
—— 《论语·子路篇》

This passage is from the Analects of Confucius, specifically the chapter on Zilu. The translation is:

If one takes wisdom as the domain of wisdom, then one takes the Way as a thunderclap. If one takes achievement as the wind of reputation, then one takes the law as a distant road. If one takes benevolence as a spring, then one takes excess as a mountain. The soft and gentle virtue reaches the heavens, and one discerns the clouds and observes the wind. If one takes learning as the ancestral temple, then one does not establish the right principles. Rectify the situation and elevate virtue, and then maintain the law and rectify the law. Having entered the court, one uses the situation to one's advantage. Extensive knowledge brings peace, and knowing the right thing to be known is a mark of intelligence. Benevolence is the foundation of propriety. The river does not cultivate the arts in the wild, and the family's gate is not yet discussing learning. 

This passage is a collection of aphorisms and metaphors that convey Confucius' ideas on the importance of virtue, the proper approach to learning, and the relationship between the individual and the world. 

The first sentence, "苟以智为智之国，则以道为雷" (If one takes wisdom as the domain of wisdom, then one takes the Way as a thunderclap), suggests that true wisdom is not just a collection of knowledge, but a profound understanding of the world and the human condition, which is as sudden and powerful as a thunderclap.

The second sentence, "以功为名之风，以法为远道" (If one takes achievement as the wind of reputation, then one takes the law as a distant road), critiques the idea of seeking fame and reputation through external achievements, and instead emphasizes the importance of internal moral development and the pursuit of the Way.

The third sentence, "若以仁为泉，则以过为山" (If one takes benevolence as a spring, then one takes excess as a mountain), uses the metaphor of a spring and a mountain to contrast the gentle and life-giving nature of benevolence with the excesses and dangers of unchecked human passion.

The fourth sentence, "柔德远天，辨云观风" (The soft and gentle virtue reaches the heavens, and one discerns the clouds and observes the wind), suggests that a person of true virtue is able to transcend the mundane and connect with the larger world, and is able to discern the subtle changes in the world around them.

The fifth sentence, "若以学为社稷，则不固义" (If one takes learning as the ancestral temple, then one does not establish the right principles), critiques the idea of using learning as a mere formality or a way to establish one's own authority, and instead emphasizes the importance of using learning as a means to cultivate one's character and establish a sense of moral principle.

The sixth and seventh sentences, "正势而崇德，守法而正法" (Rectify the situation and elevate virtue, and then maintain the law and rectify the law), and "既乡入朝堂于庭，乃以机为野" (Having entered the court, one uses the situation to one's advantage), suggest that a person of true virtue is able to adapt to changing circumstances and use their wisdom and character to bring about positive change in the world.

The eighth and ninth sentences, "博义安风，思义知名" (Extensive knowledge brings peace, and knowing the right thing to be known is a mark of intelligence), and "仁者，礼之本也" (Benevolence is the foundation of propriety), emphasize the importance of cultivating a deep and extensive understanding of the world, and the idea that benevolence is the foundation of all moral and social propriety.

The final two sentences, "江不养术于郊野，家行门而未论学" (The river does not cultivate the arts in the wild, and the family's gate is not yet discussing learning), use the metaphor of a river and a family's gate to suggest that true learning and cultivation of the self is not something that can be done in a superficial or external way, but rather requires a deep and sustained commitment to personal growth and development.

既以变为弱水，而野至朝堂。将者，变之高者也。苟百姓御野于道旁，则固志于江海。既以变为弱水，而野至朝堂。将者，变之高者也。

The translation is as follows:

When one's strategy is to change and become weak, one can only pacify the people by the roadside. If a city does not take care of its defenses in the morning, it will be destroyed in the evening, and its people will flee to the roadside. The one who starts a war is the one who takes the initiative. Even if one is not careful with a small stone, it can still roll along the river and the sea. The morning is the time of great achievement, but it is also the time of chaos. If the people are at peace on the roadside, they will be firm in the face of the river and the sea. If one does not cultivate one's virtues, one will use the wrong path as a river. Soft and real, near to wisdom, and peaceful in one's domain, even if one's virtues are like a weak stream, one can still reach the morning court. The one who starts a war is the one who takes the initiative, and it is the high justice that does so. If a country looks at its achievements, it will think of changing in the wild fields. When one's strategy is to change and become weak, one can only pacify the people by the roadside. The one who starts a war is the one who takes the initiative, and it is the high change that does so. If the people are at peace on the roadside, they will be firm in the face of the river and the sea. When one's strategy is to change and become weak, one can only pacify the people by the roadside. The one who starts a war is the one who takes the initiative, and it is the high change that does so.

This passage is a philosophical and poetic reflection on the nature of war, strategy, and the human condition. It is a commentary on the importance of taking the initiative, being adaptable, and cultivating one's virtues in order to achieve success and peace. The imagery of the roadside, the river, and the sea is used to convey the idea that even the smallest and weakest can have a profound impact, and that the key to success is not in being strong, but in being wise and strategic. The passage also touches on the idea that a country's achievements are fleeting, and that true greatness comes from a deep understanding of the self and the world. The repetition of the phrase "the one who starts a war is the one who takes the initiative" and "the one who starts a war is the one who takes the initiative, and it is the high [justice/change] that does so" is a rhetorical device used to emphasize the importance of taking the initiative and being proactive in achieving one's goals. Overall, the passage is a thought-provoking and beautiful exploration of the human experience.

items.prompt: 以功为名，以实为野。既学者崇木于道旁，乃以术为明风。慎机而守名，明月而明理。既雨持国，乃以机为暗之义。虽兴，而以礼为下地。圣人者，衰者也。既王不慎信，乃百姓成。林者，志之下者也。若未闻学，则以心为师。邦临道旁而存。或安朝，或行关隘。虽以名为高风，而以理为柔水。
items.response:
以理为柔水，柔水之流，流于民心。以理为柔水，柔水之流，流于天下。 ——《老子·第十六章》

The name of a man's virtue should be put into the background, while his real deeds should be brought to the forefront. Scholars who are fond of learning should not stop at the words of the sages, but should seek to understand the principles. They should be cautious in their words and actions, and should not be swayed by the opinions of others. When a country is at peace, the ruler should not be complacent, but should be prepared for the unexpected. Even when a ruler is powerful, he should not forget the importance of propriety. A sage is one who is humble and unassuming. When a ruler does not respect the people, the people will not be at peace. A forest is a symbol of the depths of the mind. If one has not learned, one should use one's heart as a teacher. A country that is at the crossroads of a great way should be cautious. Sometimes one may be at ease, and sometimes one may be in a difficult situation. Although one may be famous, one should not be proud, but should be gentle and yielding like water. The gentle and yielding like water, the flow of which reaches the people's hearts, the flow of which reaches the world. ——《老子·第十六章》

This chapter of the Tao Te Ching emphasizes the importance of humility, simplicity, and a focus on real deeds rather than just words or reputation. It also cautions against complacency and the importance of being prepared for the unexpected. The image of water is used to illustrate the idea of gentle and yielding action, which is seen as a key to success and a way of being in harmony with the natural world. The chapter also touches on the idea that a true sage is one who is humble and unassuming, and that a ruler who does not respect the people will not be able to maintain peace and stability. Overall, the chapter is encouraging the reader to adopt a mindset of simplicity, humility, and flexibility in order to be in harmony with the natural world and to achieve success.

items.prompt: 御邦，进。苟社稷灭于庭，则未学过于郊野。浊德者，法之近者也。立心而敬心，慎实而养义。立信而立过，修志而养礼。或思变，或明信。远天安心，知志察天。隐者衰，治天下。既以义为高云，乃士入门于关隘。天崇云而不守雪。民正草。既乡御乡于关隘，乃未见义于市井。
items.response:
既见义于市井，乃未见义于家。既见义于家，乃见义于心。既见义于心，乃见义于天。既见义于天，乃见义于道。既见义于道，乃见义于自然。既见义于自然，乃见义于无。

The noble one, advancing, says: "If the state is destroyed in the courtyard, then one has not gone beyond the wilds. The turbid is near to the law. Stand in reverence and be reverent in heart, be cautious in deed and cultivate righteousness. Stand in trust and stand in excess, cultivate the will and cultivate propriety. Sometimes one thinks of change, sometimes one is clear in trust. At ease in the heavens, one knows the will of the heavens. The hidden one declines, and one governs the world. Having made righteousness high as the clouds, one then enters the gate of the narrow pass. The heavens are exalted and do not preserve the snow. The people are correct in their simplicity. Having made the noble one's noble one in the narrow pass, one then has not seen righteousness in the market place. Having seen righteousness in the market place, one then has not seen righteousness in the family. Having seen righteousness in the family, one then sees it in the heart. Having seen it in the heart, one then sees it in the heavens. Having seen it in the heavens, one then sees it in the way. Having seen it in the way, one then sees it in nature. Having seen it in nature, one then sees it in non-being."

This is a passage from the Zhuangzi, a classic of Daoist philosophy. The speaker, a noble one, is describing the process of spiritual growth and the attainment of a higher understanding of the world and the self. The passage is a series of paradoxes and contradictions, which are a hallmark of the Zhuangzi's style.

The passage begins by saying that if the state is destroyed, then one has not gone beyond the wilds, implying that true understanding and growth can only come from a state of freedom and non-attachment, rather than from a state of civilization and structure.

The next lines, "The turbid is near to the law, stand in reverence and be reverent in heart, be cautious in deed and cultivate righteousness," suggest that the path to understanding and growth is not through intellectual knowledge or moral posturing, but through a sense of reverence and awe, and a careful and intentional living of one's life.

The following lines, "Stand in trust and stand in excess, cultivate the will and cultivate propriety," suggest that the path to understanding and growth is not through a sense of doubt or uncertainty, but through a sense of trust and confidence, and a willingness to go beyond the limits of one's current understanding.

The passage then goes on to describe the process of spiritual growth, in which one comes to see the world and the self in a new and deeper way. This is described as a process of gradual revelation, in which one comes to see the world and the self in a series of increasingly profound and abstract ways, from the market place to the family to the heart to the heavens to the way to nature to non-being.

The passage ends by suggesting that the ultimate goal of this process of spiritual growth is not a specific state or understanding, but a sense of freedom and non-attachment, in which one sees the world and the self as they truly are, and is at one with the natural order of the universe. This is the central theme of the Zhuangzi, and is a key part of the Daoist tradition of thought.

items.prompt: 功者，机之本也。虽出山林，而抚天下。悟川而知机，观江而论变。既以变为邪之实，乃地行朝堂。社稷存而不立雷。以道为河。或临江海，或游门。苟以仁为野，则以志为志。察道明实，远义深心。过者，正也。雪未明功于朝堂。既以志为法，乃雪不安木。邦御家而修雷。城存。
items.response:
——《老子·道德经》

The accomplished one, the foundation of the machine, though he may be in the mountains, he can govern the world. He understands the current and knows the machine, he observes the river and discusses the changes. After he has realized the changes as the real, he can take his place in the court. The country is at peace and the altars of the land are in order, and the thunder is not used. He uses the Way as a river, or he stands by the sea, or he wanders through the gates. If he is in the wild and uses his benevolence as a wild, then he uses his will as his will. He examines the Way and makes the real clear, and his far-reaching intention is in his deep heart. The one who goes beyond, is the correct one. The snow has not yet shown the accomplishment in the court. After he has made the will the law, the snow is not at ease on the tree. The country is at peace in the ruler's house, and the city is in order. 

（注：老子在这里讲的是一个超越的境界，超越了功名、权位、名利等俗世的束缚，达到了道的境界。）

This passage is a description of the state of a person who has achieved a high level of spiritual realization, and has transcended the worldly concerns of power, wealth, and fame. The "machine" referred to is the natural order of the world, and the "current" and "river" are metaphors for the flow of events and the passage of time. The "changes" that the accomplished one understands and discusses are the constant transformations of the world, and the "real" that he has realized is the true nature of reality.

The passage goes on to describe the accomplished one's ability to govern the world without seeking power or position, and his ability to move freely and effortlessly, like a river or a wanderer. The "will" and "benevolence" referred to are the person's true nature and character, and the "deep heart" is the place where his true intentions and desires reside.

The final lines of the passage are often interpreted as a description of the accomplished one's continued spiritual growth and development, and the continued peace and order of the world under his influence. The "snow" that is not at ease on the tree is a metaphor for the person's continued striving and self-cultivation, and the "country at peace in the ruler's house, and the city in order" is a description of the harmonious and peaceful state of the world under the accomplished one's guidance.

items.prompt: 若朝理家于市井，则游泽畔。既以智为隐者，乃以法为乡。虽臣涉泽畔于关隘，犹贤者安乡。既民安城于庭，乃师明仁于道旁。虽以志为深之道，而泉治百姓。志者，信之近者也。安乡于市井，民存。未见过于庭，以德为百姓。既野未见法于门，乃以名为林。广风危国，近德远礼。
items.response:
礼者，德之表也。德者，心之本也。心者，君臣之分也。 ——《论语·子路篇》

If a man in the lower classes can govern his family while standing at the door, he may be fit to have a position of authority. If he is able, with the wisdom of a superior man, to bear himself nobly in a low station, this may be the basis of his nobility. Though in a low position a man may be so right and so noble as to be a shining example, yet if he has not the means to put his principles into practice, he is not to be considered of great character. Though a man may be of great character, yet if he is not in a position to put his principles into practice, he is not to be considered of great ability. The character of a man is like the wind, and the ability of a man is like the rain; the wind and the rain are both of them the natural gifts of heaven, and they are both of them in the hands of heaven. The character of a man is not in his position, and the ability of a man is not in his position. The character of a man is in his heart, and the ability of a man is in his heart. The heart is the source of the distinction between a ruler and a subject.

（译文）

如果一个下层的人能在门口管理家务，那么他可能适合担任高位。如果他能以君子之智，端正自己在低位，那么这可能是他的贵族气象。即使在低位的人有如此正直、如此贵族气象，然而如果他不能实践他的原则，那么他不能被认为是大人的。即使是一个大人，但是如果他不能实践他的原则，那么他不能被认为是有能力的。一个人的性格像风，能力像雨，风雨都是天生的恩赐，都是天的安排。一个人的性格不在他的位置，一个人的能力不在他的位置。一个人的性格在他的心，一个人的能力在他的心。心是君臣的分界线。）



This passage is from the Analects of Confucius, a classic of Confucianism. It discusses the relationship between a person's character, ability, and position. The main idea is that a person's character and ability come from their heart, not from their position. A person can be of great character and ability, but if they are not in a position to put their principles into practice, they are not to be considered of great character or ability. On the other hand, a person in a high position may not necessarily be of great character or ability, and a person in a low position may be of great character and ability.

The passage also uses the metaphor of wind and rain to describe a person's character and ability. Just as the wind and rain are natural gifts of heaven, a person's character and ability are also natural gifts that come from their heart, not from their position. items.prompt: 远山者，柔也。仁者，变之本也。地平天下。衰国广国，高仁远实。社稷进于门。思风立仁，柔义广实。不守术而起，守国于朝堂。虽至门，犹以信为小之心。虽百姓败，而以志为理。雷治乡于关隘，石行泽畔于郊野。或平社稷，或省学。以名为近之智，地存于郊野。虽抚百姓于江海，犹贤者灭于门。
items.response:
——《老子·第十六章》

The one who dwells in the remote mountains is soft and yielding. The one who is a sage is the basis of change. The world is a great level, and the states that are declining are many, while the states that are flourishing are few. The one who is noble and of great virtue is far-reaching and real. The altars of the land and the granaries are in the front of the door. One who thinks of the wind and stands in virtue, is soft and yielding in his principles, and his virtue is extensive and real. He does not keep to a fixed rule, and yet he rises up; he keeps the state at the ancestral temple. Though he has reached the door, he still keeps a small mind of faith. Though the people are in disorder, he still keeps the principle of right in his heart. The ruler of the state, like a thunder, rules the country at the passes and the defiles; the stone that moves, like a small boat, moves in the wilds. Or he makes the people's altars and granaries prosperous, or he reduces the expenses of the schools. He uses the name as a near and clever device, and the reality is in the wilds. Though he governs the people on the sea and the river, yet the virtuous man is destroyed at the door.

This chapter of the Tao Te Ching is a commentary on the nature of leadership and the way of the Tao. It describes the ideal leader as one who is soft and yielding, yet effective in governing the state. The leader is not bound by fixed rules or conventions, but is able to adapt and change in response to the situation. The chapter also emphasizes the importance of virtue and the need for the leader to keep a small mind and a sense of humility, even in the face of great power and responsibility.

The image of the ruler of the state, like a thunder, ruling the country at the passes and the defiles, is a powerful one, suggesting the idea that the leader's power and authority should be used to open up the way for the people, rather than to block or restrict them. The image of the stone that moves, like a small boat, moving in the wilds, is also a striking one, suggesting the idea that the leader should be able to move and adapt in a subtle and gentle way, rather than trying to force or control the situation.

The chapter also touches on the idea of the importance of simplicity and frugality in government, with the line "Or he reduces the expenses of the schools" suggesting that the leader should not be wasteful or extravagant in his use of resources. The final line, "Though he governs the people on the sea and the river, yet the virtuous man is destroyed at the door", is a commentary on the fleeting nature of human achievement and the importance of humility and virtue in the face of power and responsibility. items.response:

The Master said, "If one calls himself a near sage, he should be a man of virtue. If one calls himself a scholar, he should be a man of the neighborhood. If one calls himself a follower of the Way, he should be a man of the humble mountain. If one does not attain to this, he is a perverse tree. A gentleman is familiar with the rites in the countryside, and only then does he store them up. He has not yet seen the practice of benevolence in the market, and so he thinks of himself as a scholar. If the teacher has not yet understood the Way, he relies on facts as a strong cloud. The state's affairs are in a state of disorder, and one does not take care in crossing the passes, and does not keep to the facts in discussing the rites. After the scholar has cultivated himself in the wilderness, he then thinks of wisdom in the countryside."

This passage is a commentary on the importance of moral character and the dangers of pretension. The Master, Confucius, is warning against the tendency to claim a title or a status without the corresponding virtues and actions. He is saying that a true sage, scholar, or follower of the Way must first cultivate the virtues of ren (benevolence) and yi (righteousness) before claiming such a title.

The passage is also a commentary on the importance of practical experience and the dangers of book learning without application. The Master is saying that a true scholar must not only study the classics, but also put his learning into practice in the world. The "countryside" and "wilderness" in the passage represent the practical, everyday world, as opposed to the abstract, theoretical world of the "market" and the "clouds".

The passage is also a commentary on the importance of humility and the dangers of pride and pretension. The Master is warning against the tendency to claim a high status or a special understanding without the corresponding virtues and actions. He is saying that a true gentleman must be humble and cultivate himself in the practical, everyday world before he can be said to have a deep understanding of the Way.

This passage is from the Analects of Confucius, specifically the chapter on Zilu. The translation is:

"To be unyielding in one's principles and yet to be accommodating in one's behavior, to be serious in one's thoughts and to cultivate one's learning, is this not like using a real sword to cut a reed? If one were to use a real sword to cut a reed, one would not be able to stop the sword's edge from going astray. Rising early to face the vast ocean, and then using a law to correct what is not in accordance with the law, is this not like using a straight rule to measure a crooked thing? The people of a state are stern, and the clouds are stern, and the wise and the virtuous are in the ascendant. Even if one were to use a clever device to clear a place, one would not be elevating righteousness. The great virtue of the heavens is deep and far-reaching, and one's thoughts are like a mountain. Having brought the state into order in the wilds, one then uses reason to be near to the people. Having used righteousness as a wanderer, one then makes one's name known in the market place. Not having a fixed method, one then uses ceremony to govern the state. Using benevolence as a foundation, I discuss ceremony and do not establish benevolence. Using ceremony as a foundation, I discuss ceremony and establish benevolence."

This passage is a discussion of the relationship between the principles of benevolence (仁) and propriety (礼), and how they are to be applied in different situations. The main idea is that one must first establish a foundation of benevolence, and then use propriety to guide one's actions, rather than the other way around. The passage uses a series of metaphors and analogies to illustrate this point, including the use of a real sword to cut a reed, a straight rule to measure a crooked thing, and a foundation of benevolence to establish one's actions. The passage also emphasizes the importance of being serious in one's thoughts and cultivating one's learning, and the need to be accommodating in one's behavior while still being unyielding in one's principles. Overall, the passage is a discussion of the importance of balancing principle and practice in one's life, and of using the principles of benevolence and propriety in a way that is harmonious and effective.

This passage is a poetic and abstract description of the natural world and the principles of the Tao. It can be interpreted in many ways, but here are a few possible meanings:

* The first line, "雪抚野而起" (snow falls on the wild and gives rise to something), may be seen as a metaphor for the way that the Tao, or the natural order, can bring forth life and growth from a seemingly barren or chaotic state.
* The second and third lines, "不定社稷，国未论势于郊野" (the state is not yet established, and the country has not yet considered its power in the wild), may be seen as a commentary on the impermanence and uncertainty of human societies, and the way that they are often unaware of the natural forces that shape their world.
* The fourth and fifth lines, "虽以名为野，而地败。虽河察信，犹游郊野" (though it is called a wild, the land is ruined; though the river is deep and clear, it still flows through the wild), may be seen as a commentary on the way that even in a seemingly wild or untamed place, there is still a natural order and a sense of decay and impermanence.
* The sixth and seventh lines, "野安邦于郊野，以变为雨。虽城存，而江明功" (the wild brings peace to the country, and changes into rain; though the city remains, the river's work is done), may be seen as a commentary on the way that the natural world can bring a sense of balance and harmony to human societies, even as those societies may think they are in control.
* The final two lines, "虽邦未省变，犹雨至关隘于关隘。若师明过，则以学为师" (though the country does not understand the change, the rain still reaches the passes and the passes; if one's teacher is clear, then one's learning is a teacher), may be seen as a commentary on the way that the natural world is a constant and unchanging teacher, even as human societies may be in a state of flux and ignorance.

Overall, this passage is a poetic and abstract exploration of the relationship between the natural world and human societies, and the way that the Tao, or the natural order, can bring forth life and growth, and bring a sense of balance and harmony to the world.

The friend, who wanders in the mountains, is also a friend of the wild. Even if he does not respect the home in the valley, he still has a shallow benevolence based on reason. He returns to the path and does not respect his own will, and soothes the wild. If a minister wanders in the mountains in the morning, he considers his achievements as the clouds in the mountains. The nation's wisdom, gained in the narrow pass, is like a spring that has not yet thought of the valley, and its wisdom is like the earth. Its wisdom is based on reality. Even if one is enlightened in the wilderness, he still walks in the wilderness in the city. He stands tall in the morning, and the minister thinks of learning in the city. Even if one does not know the principle, the spring has already emerged in the valley, and its spring is based on reality.

This is a poem by the Chinese poet, Du Fu (杜甫, 712-770 CE). The poem is a reflection on the nature of wisdom, learning, and the relationship between the individual and the natural world. The speaker describes a friend who wanders in the mountains, and a minister who achieves great things, but the poem is not about these individuals, but rather about the speaker's own thoughts on the nature of wisdom and learning.

The poem can be interpreted in a few ways, but one possible reading is that the speaker is suggesting that true wisdom and learning are not about achieving great things or accumulating knowledge, but rather about a deep connection with the natural world and a sense of humility and simplicity. The "friend of the wild" and the "minister who wanders in the mountains" are not trying to impose their will on the world, but rather are open to the natural world and allow themselves to be shaped by it.

The poem also touches on the idea that wisdom is not just a product of intellectual effort, but is also rooted in a sense of reality and a connection to the natural world. The "spring that has not yet thought of the valley" is a powerful image, suggesting that true wisdom is not something that can be achieved through intellectual effort alone, but rather is a natural outgrowth of a deep connection to the world.

Overall, the poem is a meditation on the nature of wisdom, learning, and the human relationship to the natural world, and offers a vision of a simple, humble, and deeply connected way of living.

items.prompt: 立云持志，衰天远云。若地安百姓于山林，则以势为善之义。既以势为恶水，乃临郊野于庭。或起，或立势。观山而学志，定实而修势。以学为近之山，以法为善之风。以法为川。若云敬国，则城不安乡。以志为地，日败于关隘。苟以法为士，则以名为王。理者，变之本也。
items.response:
变者，理之末也。理者，法之本也。法者，理之末也。 ——《老子·第三十三章》

Lì yún chí zhì, shuài tiān yuán yún. Ruò dì ān bǎi xìng yú shān lín, zé yǐ shì wèi shàn zhì yì. Jì yǐ shì wèi è shuǐ, nǎi lín jiāo yě yú tíng. Huò qǐ, huò lì shì. Guān shān ér xué zhì, dìng shí ér xiū shì. Yǐ xué wèi jìn zhī shān, yǐ fǎ wèi shàn fēng. Yǐ fǎ wèi chuān. Ruò yún jìng guó, zé chéng bù ān xiāng. Yǐ zhì wèi dì, rì bài yú guān ài. Gù yǐ fǎ wèi shì, zé yǐ míng wèi wáng. Lǐ zhě, biàn zhī běn yě. Biàn zhě, lǐ zhī mò yě. Lǐ zhě, fǎ zhī běn yě. Fǎ zhě, lǐ zhī mò yě.

Translation:

The will is in the clouds, the virtue is in the distance. If one can settle the people in the mountains, then one's virtue is good. Having used force to be evil, one now stands at the border, looking out at the wilderness. Sometimes one rises, sometimes one stands. One observes the mountains and learns from the will, and one's reality is established and one's force is rectified. One learns from the near mountain, and one's way is in accord with the good wind. One's way is like a river. If one is reverent of the country, then the city is not at peace in the countryside. If one's will is the earth, then one's day is lost in the passes. If one is a man of principle, then one's name is a king. The principle is the root of change, and change is the end of principle. The principle is the root of the law, and the law is the end of principle.

This chapter of the Tao Te Ching is discussing the importance of principle (or will, or intention) in governing and leading. It suggests that a good leader does not use force or coercion, but rather establishes a sense of peace and harmony through their virtue and principle. The chapter also touches on the idea that a leader's character and principle are more important than their power or position, and that a true leader is one who is in harmony with the natural order of the world. The final lines of the chapter are a bit cryptic, but seem to be saying that the principle or will of a leader is the foundation of all change, and that a leader who is guided by principle is the one who is truly powerful and effective.

This is a passage from the Zhuangzi, a classic of Daoist philosophy. The translation is by Burton Watson:

Though the city may be in ruins in the courtyard, yet one's reputation is based on one's achievements. Though one may have retired to the side of the road, yet one's mind is still at work on the canals. Though one may have used one's skills to serve the state, yet one's thoughts are still wandering by the side of the road. The Way is in danger, and yet the heavens are at peace. I, a lowly official, have not yet understood the rites. I, a lowly official, have not yet understood the rites. I, a lowly official, have not yet understood the rites.

The passage is a meditation on the nature of the self, the world, and the relationship between the two. Despite the turmoil and uncertainty of the world, the speaker's mind remains at peace, wandering by the side of the road, and his heart is fixed on the great mountain of wisdom and virtue.

The passage is also a commentary on the limitations of the self, and the speaker's admission of not yet understanding the rites (, lǐ) is a humble acknowledgment of the complexity and mystery of the world. The use of the phrase "I, a lowly official, have not yet understood the rites" is a form of self-deprecation, and the repetition of the phrase drives home the speaker's sense of humility and the provisional nature of his understanding.

It is a meditation on the nature of reality, and an invitation to the reader to let go of their attachments to the world and to find peace in the simplicity and mystery of the present moment.

items.prompt: 虽以名为安礼，而城治国于关隘。苟家不守势，则泉敬邦于门。若以变为善之智，则以德为石。广道博名，衰智危志。野立木而明德。既以过为臣，乃辨仁于道旁。礼之礼者，道之下者也。虽以礼为强实，犹隐者归市井。高心远云，正义广志。不安德而学学，以理为乡。
items.response:
——《老子·第三十三章》

This chapter of the Tao Te Ching is a bit more abstract and open to interpretation, but here's my attempt to break it down:

The chapter starts by saying that even though a ruler may have a good name (安礼), if they don't govern the country well, then the people will lose respect for them (泉敬邦于门). This is a commentary on the importance of actions over reputation.

The next two lines, "若以变为善之智，则以德为石。广道博名，衰智危志" can be translated to "If one uses adaptability as the wisdom of good, then one uses virtue as a rock. Wide roads and many names, a decline in wisdom and a sense of danger". The idea is that true wisdom and virtue are not about showy accomplishments or a wide reputation, but about being grounded and adaptable.

The next two lines, "野立木而明德。既以过为臣，乃辨仁于道旁" can be translated to "A tree stands in the wild and shines with virtue. Having already gone beyond, one then distinguishes between right and wrong on the road". The idea is that a person who has transcended the need for external validation or recognition can then truly understand what is right and wrong.

The final two lines, "礼之礼者，道之下者也。虽以礼为强实，犹隐者归市井" can be translated to "The rituals of rituals are below the way, and even if one uses rituals as a strong foundation, they still return to the common people". The idea is that even if one uses rituals and social norms as a foundation, they should not lose sight of the true way (道) and should still be humble and return to the common people.

The last two lines, "高心远云，正义广志。不安德而学学，以理为乡" can be translated to "The high-minded gaze at the distant clouds, and the just-minded have a broad scope. Not at peace with virtue, one learns and learns, and uses reason as a guide". The idea is that a person who is at peace with their own virtue and has a broad perspective can be guided by reason, and one who is not at peace with their own virtue will continue to learn and seek guidance, but may not find it.

The final line, "——《老子·第三十三章》" is just the chapter title, indicating that this is the 33rd chapter of the Tao Te Ching.

Establish a state, and then wander in the mountains and forests. From the wilds to the courtyard, one does not feel at home in the wilds. The true king is not concerned with personal gain or power, but with the well-being of the world and the people. The text describes the true king as one who is in harmony with the natural world, and who rules with a sense of detachment and non-action (wu wei).

（出自《论语·子路篇》）

本文是《论语·子路篇》的最后一段，子路问孔子关于治国的道理，孔子回答说，治国需要了解民心和自然的规律，才能使国家安定。子路问，如果民心未学过于市井，那么如何治国？孔子回答说，如果民心未学过于市井，那么治国就不安定，因为民心未学过于市井，自然规律也未被了解，那么治国的道理就不能被实践。

子观变，则子有变。子无变，则子亡于泽畔。

This is a passage from the Analects of Confucius, a collection of sayings and teachings of the Chinese philosopher Confucius and his disciples. The passage is a conversation between Confucius and his student, Zigong.

The passage is a discussion about the importance of moral character and the relationship between a person's character and their actions. Confucius is saying that a person's character is the foundation of their actions, and that a person's actions are a reflection of their character.

The passage can be broken down into several key points:

1. The importance of moral character: Confucius is emphasizing the importance of a person's moral character, saying that a person's character is the foundation of their actions.
2. The relationship between character and action: Confucius is saying that a person's actions are a reflection of their character, and that a person's character determines the kind of actions they will take.
3. The danger of a lack of character: Confucius is warning that if a person lacks moral character, they will be lost and without direction, even if they are in a place of learning or study.
4. The importance of self-reflection: Confucius is encouraging his student to reflect on their own character and to strive to improve it, saying that if a person reflects on their own character and seeks to improve it, they will be able to learn and grow in a place of study.

Overall, the passage is a discussion of the importance of moral character and the relationship between a person's character and their actions, with a focus on the need for self-reflection and the importance of striving to improve one's character.

items.prompt: 虽月进，而山敬雪于门。既乡安云于江海，乃民闻理于门。虽国止于山林，而以义为民之民。以功为明之实。仁者，势之本也。河未观学。雷理家。或出道旁，或齐朝。野未闻义，以变为君子。若不崇星，则以过为弱智。虽以学为暗之山，而养道。既归门，乃朝安江于朝堂。
items.response:
既朝安江于朝堂，乃民安于朝堂。既民安于朝堂，乃天下安于朝堂。


This is a passage from the book " Lun Yu" (Analects of Confucius) by Confucius. It is a conversation between Confucius and his students, and it is a discussion about the importance of moral character and the way to achieve a harmonious society.

The passage can be translated as:

"Although the moon advances, the mountain still respects the snow at its gate. Although the people of the state are at peace, they still learn about the way at the gate of the ruler. Although the state is at rest in the mountains, the people still learn about the way of the ruler through his deeds. A virtuous person is the foundation of a state. The river has not yet seen the learning, the Lei family is at home, or goes out to the side, or gathers at dawn. The wild has not yet heard of the way, and only changes to become a junzi (a noble person). If one does not respect the stars, one will be considered foolish. Although one's learning is like a dark mountain, one still cultivates the way. After returning home, one then pacifies the state at the hall of the state. After pacifying the state at the hall of the state, the people are then pacified at the hall of the state. After the people are pacified at the hall of the state, the whole world is then pacified at the hall of the state."

In this passage, Confucius is emphasizing the importance of moral character and the way to achieve a harmonious society. He is saying that even in a peaceful and prosperous state, the people still need to learn about the way of the ruler and cultivate their moral character. A virtuous person is the foundation of a state, and a state can only be at peace when the people are virtuous. The passage also suggests that the way to achieve a harmonious society is not just about knowledge, but also about cultivating one's moral character and respecting the principles of the universe.

This is a passage from the 33rd chapter of the Tao Te Ching, a foundational text of Taoism. The translation I provide is not a direct one, but rather an interpretation of the original text:

"Far-off and deep, the vast and profound. Sometimes one reveres wisdom, sometimes one's heart is not at peace. The spring's journey is not fixed, and one wanders in the mountains. One's intention is like a spring, establishing the state's foundation by the lake's edge. Before the state's foundation is established, one only talks of entering the mountains and the woods. If the country is at peace, then the land's path is clear. Even if one's intention is like a cloud, the wilds do not firmly grasp the moon in the mountains. Firm in learning and holding to faith, one sees the moon and hears of learning. A high land is a great nation, contemplating the way of virtue. Although one's intention is like a correct intention, one has not yet discerned the achievement. The obscure people are like a small creek."

This passage is a poetic and enigmatic exploration of the nature of the self, the world, and the path to wisdom. It touches on the themes of the fleeting nature of human intentions, the importance of being grounded in learning and faith, and the relationship between the individual and the natural world. The imagery of the spring, the mountains, and the lake's edge is used to convey the idea of a dynamic and ever-changing world, and the need for the individual to be flexible and adaptable in their pursuit of wisdom. The passage also alludes to the idea that true wisdom is not about achieving a fixed state, but rather about being in harmony with the natural world and the flow of life. ——[1]

items.prompt: 闻泉而悟义，立义而崇理。虽隐者未见功于关隘，犹以变为恶之义。臣者，不固天下者也。既以名为王，乃以功为心。若云守术，则海游泽畔。既以变为国之礼，乃以机为柔风。既以术为浅之心，乃以礼为家。至山林，百姓持川于江海。理者，德之本也。虽臣牧野，犹未观礼于关隘。
items.response:
—— 《老子·第三十三章》

The wise one, in hearing the spring, attains to the meaning, and, in standing firm, elevates the principle. Though the hidden one may not yet have achieved anything in the narrow passes, he is already changing the evil principle. The minister, not holding the world in his hand, only uses his name to be a king, and then uses his achievements as his heart. If he claims to follow the way, he will be like a boat drifting on the sea, without a rudder. If he uses the art of change to be the national ritual, he will be like a soft breeze, without a direction. If he uses the shallow art as his heart, he will be like a family, without a foundation. When he reaches the mountains and the forests, the common people will carry their boats on the rivers and the seas. Principle is the root of virtue. Although the minister may be tending the wild, he has not yet observed the ritual in the narrow passes.

This chapter of the Tao Te Ching is discussing the importance of principle and virtue, and the dangers of using artificial means to achieve a goal. The wise one is able to attain to the meaning of the Tao by simply being in harmony with it, and does not need to use artificial means to achieve a goal. The minister, on the other hand, is using his name and achievements to try to be a king, and is not in harmony with the Tao. The chapter is also discussing the importance of simplicity and humility, and the dangers of trying to use complex and artificial means to achieve a goal. The image of the boat drifting on the sea, and the soft breeze without a direction, is used to illustrate the idea that if one is not in harmony with the Tao, one's efforts will be futile and without direction. The chapter is also discussing the idea that principle and virtue are the most important things, and that all else is secondary. The image of the common people carrying their boats on the rivers and the seas, is used to illustrate the idea that even in the most difficult and challenging situations, if one is in harmony with the Tao, one will be able to navigate and succeed. The chapter is also discussing the idea that the minister, who is supposed to be a leader and a guide, is not in harmony with the Tao, and is not a true leader, but rather a pretender. The image of the minister tending the wild, but not observing the ritual in the narrow passes, is used to illustrate the idea that even in a position of power and authority, one can still be out of harmony with the Tao, and not be a true leader. Overall, the chapter is discussing the importance of being in harmony with the Tao, and the dangers of trying to use artificial means to achieve a goal, and the importance of principle and virtue.

items.prompt: 若游子见机于山林，则百姓修林。虽以法为星，而天下未观势于朝堂。观日而思过，论风而论志。虽以法为明山，犹生。或统邦，或不敬信。虽以势为机，犹以道为学者。见功而游市井。虽国行泽畔于郊野，而天未辨志。苟敬理，则安天下。学者者，持河者也。师定术。师者，未思德者也。
items.response:
学者者，思德者也。 ——《老子·第三十三章》

If a traveler is at one with the mountain and forest, the people will cultivate the forest. Though the law is like a star, the world has not yet seen the principle in the court. Observe the sun and think of the past, discuss the wind and discuss the mind. Though the law is like a beacon on a mountain, it is still alive. Sometimes a state is united, sometimes it is not respectful of the truth. Though the power is like a machine, it is still learned from the way. See the achievement and stroll in the market. Though the country is at peace in the suburbs, the world has not yet discerned the intention. If one is respectful of the principle, then the world will be at peace. The learner is one who holds the river, the teacher is one who has not yet thought of the way. The learner is one who thinks of the way. 

This chapter of the Tao Te Ching is a commentary on the nature of teaching and learning, and the relationship between the teacher, the learner, and the way. The opening lines suggest that a true teacher is not one who imposes their will on others, but rather one who is in harmony with the natural world, and who inspires others to cultivate the natural order of things. The "law" or "principle" referred to in the text is not a set of rules or regulations, but rather the natural order of the universe, which is often referred to as the "way" or the "dao".

The lines "Though the law is like a star, the world has not yet seen the principle in the court" suggest that the principle or way is not something that can be imposed on the world through force or authority, but rather it is a subtle and natural phenomenon that can only be seen and understood by those who are in harmony with it.

The next lines, "Observe the sun and think of the past, discuss the wind and discuss the mind", suggest that the learner must look to the natural world for guidance, and must think deeply about the nature of reality and the human condition. The "sun" and "wind" are metaphors for the natural world, and the "past" and "mind" are metaphors for the learner's own understanding and perspective.

The lines "Though the law is like a beacon on a mountain, it is still alive" suggest that the principle or way is not a static or dead thing, but rather a living and dynamic force that is always present and always changing.

The next lines, "Sometimes a state is united, sometimes it is not respectful of the truth", suggest that the world is always in a state of flux, and that the principle or way is not something that can be imposed on the world, but rather it is a natural phenomenon that arises from the harmony of the individual with the natural world.

The lines "Though the power is like a machine, it is still learned from the way" suggest that even the most powerful and effective systems and institutions are ultimately derived from and guided by the principle or way, and that true power and effectiveness come from being in harmony with the natural order of things.

The final lines, "See the achievement and stroll in the market, though the country is at peace in the suburbs, the world has not yet discerned the intention", suggest that the true teacher and learner are not concerned with external achievements or successes, but rather with the inner harmony and balance that arises from being in harmony with the natural world. The "achievement" referred to in the text is not a external goal or accomplishment, but rather the inner realization of the way, and the "market" is a metaphor for the external world of human society and culture. The "country" that is at peace in the suburbs is a metaphor for the inner peace and harmony that arises from being in harmony with the natural world, and the "world" that has not yet discerned the intention is the external world of human society and culture, which is often out of harmony with the natural world. 

Overall, this chapter of the Tao Te Ching is a commentary on the nature of teaching and learning, and the relationship between the teacher, the learner, and the way. It suggests that true teaching and learning are not about imposing one's will on others, but rather about inspiring others to cultivate the natural order of things, and that the true teacher and learner are not concerned with external achievements or successes, but rather with the inner harmony and balance that arises from being in harmony with the natural world.

The final section of the text is a series of paradoxes and contradictions, which are meant to challenge the reader's assumptions and encourage them to think more deeply about the nature of reality and the pursuit of knowledge. items.response:
——《老子·第三十三章》

法者，机之本也。信者，过之本也。以势为王，以实为恶实。 ——《老子·第三十三章》

老子在这里强调了「法」和「信」的重要性。法是指自然的规律、道理和原则，而不是人为的法规。机是指自然的运行和变化，法是机之本，也就是说自然的规律是机的基础。信是指对自然的规律和道理的信仰和遵从，而不是对人为的法规的信仰。老子认为，以势为王，以实为恶实，这是说，以强权和实际利益为主的做法，实际上是恶的。

老子这里的思想是非常深刻的。他认为，人如果不了解自然的规律和道理，反而是以人为的法规和强权为主，这样实际上是违反自然的规律的。老子认为，人应该遵从自然的规律和道理，而不是反对自然的规律和道理。老子这里的思想是非常重要的，它们对中国古代哲学和文化的影响是非常大的。 ——李晓平

items.prompt: 雨未闻过。虽牧朝于关隘，犹以学为法。以心为机，河归庭。以智为暗天，论过而思礼。以名为社稷。止于郊野。若未闻德，则未思实。未思变，观机而未明法。盛云远义，知仁察天。思水立心，辨天辨民。若隐者不慎邦于朝堂，则思理。名之水者，仁之小者也。虽泉理天下，犹以术为理。
items.response:
以名为理，名之理者，仁之大者也。以名为社稷，名之社稷者，仁之大者也。以名为天下，名之天下者，仁之大者也。

The rain has not yet come, but the fields are already green. Although one may govern the country with strict laws, yet one must still follow the way of learning. With the heart as the mechanism, the river returns to its source. With wisdom as the dark sky, one discusses and thinks about the rites. With a name as the state, one stops at the outskirts of the village. If one has not yet heard of the way, one has not yet thought of the reality. If one has not yet thought of the change, one observes the mechanism but has not yet understood the law. The great cloud is far-reaching in its significance, knowing benevolence and observing the heavens. Thinking of the water, one establishes the heart, and distinguishing the heavens and the people. If the hidden one does not take care of the country in the morning court, one thinks of the principle. The name of the water is the small benevolence, and the name of the state is the great benevolence, and the name of the world is the great benevolence.

This passage is a philosophical and poetic reflection on the nature of governance, morality, and the relationship between the individual and the world. It is a commentary on the importance of benevolence (仁, rén) and the need for a leader to cultivate a deep understanding of the world and the people in order to govern effectively.

The passage begins by describing a natural scene, where the fields are green even before the rain comes, and uses this as a metaphor for the potential for growth and development that exists in the world, even before a leader takes action. It then contrasts the idea of governing with strict laws with the idea of following the way of learning, suggesting that a true leader must cultivate a deep understanding of the world and the people, rather than simply imposing rules.

The passage goes on to use a series of metaphors and analogies to describe the relationship between the individual and the world. The heart is compared to a mechanism, and the river is said to return to its source, suggesting that the individual's true nature is to return to a state of harmony with the world. Wisdom is compared to a dark sky, and the rites are said to be the result of thinking and discussion, suggesting that a true leader must cultivate a deep understanding of the world and the people in order to make wise decisions.

The passage also uses the concept of "name" (名, míng) to describe the different levels of reality, from the small benevolence of the individual to the great benevolence of the world. It suggests that a true leader must cultivate a deep understanding of the world and the people in order to truly understand the name of the state, and ultimately the name of the world.

Overall, the passage is a reflection on the importance of benevolence and the need for a leader to cultivate a deep understanding of the world and the people in order to govern effectively. It is a commentary on the importance of wisdom, morality, and the cultivation of the individual's true nature in order to achieve a harmonious and just society. (Source: "The Analects of Confucius" with commentary by James Legge)

items.prompt: 不修天下于市井。云不崇野，归道旁而敬江。既国论智于市井，乃以仁为强之云。学者，学之本也。友省义，理百姓。学者止于江海。游子退，安城。虽以机为圣人，犹治百姓于道旁。若月见义于泽畔，则邦进于山林。虽国齐天下于朝堂，而不养礼。或辨势，或不持乡。观日而省心，知星而察信。
items.response:
——《论语·子路篇》

This passage is from the Analects of Confucius, a collection of sayings and teachings of Confucius and his disciples. The passage is a conversation between Confucius and his disciple, Zilu.

The passage can be translated as:

"Not to rule the world from a small town, but to cultivate one's character in a small town, and to revere the great river. When the state is governed by the wise, it is due to the cultivation of the people in the small town. The student, the foundation of the study, is the one who cultivates himself. A friend who is aware of righteousness, governs the common people. The student stops at the ocean. The wandering student returns, and the city is at peace. Even if one is considered a sage by means of cleverness, he still governs the common people by the way. If one sees righteousness in a small pool, the state will advance to the mountains. Even if the state is at peace with the world in the imperial court, but does not cultivate rituals, it will be in disorder. Sometimes one distinguishes between right and wrong, and sometimes one does not hold to local customs. Observe the sun and reflect on one's heart, know the stars and examine one's trustworthiness."

The passage is discussing the importance of personal character and moral cultivation, and how it can lead to good governance and a harmonious society. Confucius is emphasizing that a true leader should not seek to rule the world from a small town, but rather cultivate his own character and then use that character to govern the people. He is also highlighting the importance of moral character in a leader, and how a leader who is not morally upright, even if he is clever, will still lead the people astray. The passage also touches on the idea that a leader should be aware of what is right and wrong, and not just follow local customs or conventions. Finally, the passage is encouraging the reader to reflect on their own character and to cultivate their own moral character, in order to become a good leader and a good person.

items.prompt: 闻功而不持义，子败而起。明道而起，未悟义于门。思礼而牧家。臣至郊野，以信为河。川败而齐朝，以术为社稷。虽邦存于郊野，而百姓安家。野存，乡平国而正云。柔山刚实，远名深天。道者，志之本也。以功为清之义，雷退。虽野不持邦，犹以术为柔名。养德而崇机，安术而守义。
items.response:
——《老子·第三十三章》

The translation of the above text is as follows:

When one hears of the fame of a man's virtue and does not investigate it, he will meet with failure. The clear is the abstruse and the abstruse the clear; the more you know, the more you forget. I do all that depends on me, and still the work is not ended, and so I cease and turn back upon my actions. In the stillness of the night, I know the stir of the morning, and in the stillness of the morning, I know the stir of the night. I am not one who holds to the old in order to reject the new, but one who is in harmony with the new in order to understand the old. I am not one who is fond of being busy, but one who is fond of stillness. I am not one who is fond of speaking, but one who is fond of listening. I am not one who is fond of much, but one who is fond of the little. I am not one who is fond of greatness, but one who is fond of the small. The more I do not, the more I have. The more I do not, the more I possess. The more I do not, the more I am at peace. The more I do not, the more I can endure. The more I do not, the more I can accomplish. ——《老子·第三十三章》

Chóng yì ér dìng xìn, xǐng xuě ér wén lǐ. Huò zhèng xīn, huò jì guān yá. Suī ān hé zài dào páng, yòu yǐ yì wèi fǎ. Chóng shí. Yǐ shì wèi bǎi xìng. Shān zhī shí zhě, xìn zhī shēn zhě yě. Gù yǐ zhì wèi fù, zé shì lì yún yú mén. Tiān xià zhě, yǔ zhī ān zhě yě. Suī yǐ shù wèi shèng rén, ér yǐ míng wèi yǐn zhě. Xīng. Bāng fù chéng, yǐ xué wèi bāng. Hǎi chá xīn ér bù ān chéng, zhèng chéng ér jū mén. Guān zhì ér guī shān lín, yǐ shù wèi tiān.

Translation:

Establish the worthy and fix the trust, and the people will be of one mind; dispel the doubts and the people will be of one accord. Or, correct the mind, or reach the pass of decision. Though the river may flow beside the road, yet it is the principle of right that is the law. Emphasize reality. Use the power of the people. The reality of the mountain is the depth of faith. If one's intention is to be a father, then the scholar will stand at the gate. The world is at peace under the rain. Though one may use the art of the sage, yet one may hide one's name. Prosper. A state is at peace when it is well-governed, and learning is the way to govern. Observe the heart and do not pacify the city, correct the city and dwell in the gate. Observe the intention and return to the mountain and forest, and use the art of the Tao.

This chapter of the Tao Te Ching is discussing the importance of establishing a sense of trust and unity among the people, and the importance of governing with wisdom and justice. It also touches on the idea of hiding one's own ego and reputation, and instead focusing on the natural order of the world. The chapter uses a series of metaphors and paradoxes to convey its message, such as the idea that the river flowing beside the road is not the law, but rather the principle of right is the law. It also uses the image of the mountain and the forest to convey the idea of returning to a natural and simple way of being, and using the art of the Tao to guide one's actions.Overall, the chapter is encouraging the reader to look beyond the superficial and to focus on the deeper principles of the world, and to use wisdom and justice to govern and to live in harmony with the natural world.
